title: Power BI - Row-Level Security, Workspaces, And Apps
applies_to: Power BI access control and distribution planning
Power BI workspace, app, and row-level security design

confirmed_facts.fact: Row-level security in Power BI can restrict data access for specific users by using filters within roles on a semantic model.
RLS can be defined in Power BI Desktop and then managed in the Power BI service after publishing.
Workspaces are collaborative containers in the Power BI service for reports, dashboards, semantic models, and other items.
Apps are packaged, read-only collections of Power BI content distributed from a workspace to consumers.
Workspace roles determine what members can do inside a workspace, while apps provide a controlled distribution mechanism for consumers.

implementation_notes: Use RLS when the same report and semantic model must serve different audiences with different data visibility.
Design workspaces for authoring and collaboration, then publish apps for broader consumer distribution.
Treat workspace membership and app audience as separate concerns. Workspace roles govern who can build or manage content; app audiences govern who can consume curated content.
When a report request includes sensitive regional, customer, or business-unit scoping, ask whether RLS is required before calling the model complete.
caveats: RLS is model-level security, not a replacement for careful workspace governance.
A well-designed app still depends on the underlying workspace and semantic model permissions being correct.
Governance failures often come from mixing author access and consumer access without a clear workspace-plus-app strategy.
